title: Pine display box with sliding lid
subtitle: A small pine display box with a fitted sliding wooden lid
excerpt: A small pine display box 200 mm long, 100 mm wide, and 60 mm deep, with a sliding lid running in grooves cut at the top edges of the side walls. The box is glued and pinned; the lid fits with a small pull block. No complex joinery: this is a marking-gauge, saw, and chisel project in Scots or Parana pine.
sourceNotes: Victorian domestic craft manuals for small wooden boxes; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for joinery and box-making techniques.

glossaryTerms.definition: The width of the cut made by a saw blade; the material removed by the teeth. A standard tenon saw kerf is about 1 mm. Always place the saw cut on the waste side of the marked line to preserve the correct dimension.
A step cut into the edge or face of a board to accept a panel or lid. In this box, the grooves that carry the sliding lid are rebates cut into the inner face of the side walls.

content.text: Keep both hands behind the cutting edge. Secure the workpiece in a vice or with a bench hook before sawing; clamp before chiselling.

A sliding-lid box is the most direct introduction to basic joinery: all the operations, saw to the line, chisel to the mark, plane flat, fit the part, appear here in a small, forgiving scale. The critical measurement is the width of the groove that carries the lid; the
of a tenon saw is close to 1 mm, which is also the correct groove width for a well-fitting lid in 6 mm pine. Two saw cuts side by side, waste chiselled out, and the groove is done.

Scots pine or Parana pine, planed all round. Two side walls: 200 mm by 60 mm by 9 mm. Two end walls: 100 mm by 60 mm by 9 mm. One base: 200 mm by 100 mm by 6 mm. One sliding lid: 200 mm by 98 mm by 6 mm. One pull block: 25 mm square by 6 mm.
Mark the groove position on the inner face of each side wall with a marking gauge: 6 mm down from the top edge, 1 mm wide, and 3 mm deep. The groove is a

cut along the length of each side. Make two saw cuts to define the width of the groove, then chop and pare out the waste with the bench chisel. The lid should slide freely but without rattle; test with the lid blank before final assembly.
Glue and pin the four walls together, checking square with the combination square at each corner.
Glue and pin the base to the bottom of the assembled walls. Clamp flat and allow to set fully before fitting the lid.
Plane the lid to slide easily in the grooves. Fit from the open end; the lid should travel the full length without sticking.
Glue the pull block to the outer face of one end of the lid, centred. When dry, plane the pull block flush with the side walls when the lid is closed.

Smooth all outer faces with a block plane and then 120-grit sandpaper, working with the grain. Finish with two coats of oil or wax. Leave the lid unfinished until after fitting; a coat of oil swells the wood slightly and can cause a previously sliding lid to bind.

items.cause: Oil or wax finish applied to the lid before the fit was confirmed; the finish swells the wood slightly in the grooves
items.fix: Remove the lid, plane a shaving from each long edge, and test again before re-finishing. In future, fit the lid dry and sand all sliding surfaces lightly before applying any finish.
